title: R’a-beni-lütfen
text:
Enes Furkan Altın 2220469011 (github)
Mehmet Öksüzoğlu 2220469036 (github)
Arda Yeniçulha 2220469061 (github)

Project Title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices.
Project Description: This study aims to investigate the correlation between USD/TRY exchange rate movements and the performance of various sectoral indices in Turkey. We are specifically analyzing whether currency volatility serves as a significant driver for returns in the BIST 100, Electricity (XU:ELEKT), and Industrial (XU:SIN) sectors. #Data Set This study is built upon a comprehensive financial dataset spanning a 20-year period from January 2006 to May 2026. The primary variables analyzed include:
USD/TRY Exchange Rate: The core indicator used to measure currency volatility and macroeconomic stability.
BIST 100 Index (XU100.IS): The main benchmark for the overall performance of the Turkish equity market.
Sectoral Indices: BIST Banking (XBANK.IS) and BIST Industrials (XUSIN.IS) were selected to evaluate varying sectoral sensitivities to currency shocks.
Source & Selection: While the team initially attempted to utilize the Central Bank (TCMB) EVDS system, we pivoted to Yahoo Finance due to the technical complexities of EVDS API integration for a two-decade scope.
Data Preprocessing: Raw time series were cleaned through inner joins to ensure aligned trading days, filtered for missing values (NAs), and transformed into daily log returns for robust statistical analysis. #Key Takeaways Long-Term Comparative Scope: This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and Borsa Istanbul (BIST) indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to identify structural shifts in market behavior.
The 2011–2020 Depreciation Cycle: During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (as shown by the USD/TRY parity doubling and tripling) was generally correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, with the Banking sector consistently being the most vulnerable to exchange rate shocks.
Structural Divergence (2021–2026): In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite the USD/TRY parity increasing six-fold (from 100 to over 600), BIST indices provided a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns for the first time in a decade.
Sectoral Sensitivity and Resilience: While the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) typically offered more stability across all cycles, the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) displayed extreme volatility but showed a surprising recovery in USD terms during the 2021–2026 period.
Investment Implications: The analysis concludes that while exchange rate spikes historically triggered market sell-offs, the current economic regime has transformed the Turkish equity market into a primary vehicle for capital preservation against extreme currency volatility.

title: Analysis
text:
20-Year Historical Scope: This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and BIST indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to detect structural shifts.
The Regime-Dependent Banking Pivot (Your Core Insight): The Banking sector (XBANK.IS) exhibits a strong regime-dependent behavior. Under stable/tight monetary regimes where the exchange rate is controlled, Banking significantly outperforms other sectors due to predictable margins. Conversely, during rapid currency depreciation or highly volatile exchange rate regimes, Banking severely underperforms and lags behind other indices due to balance sheet shocks.
The 2011–2020 Depreciation Drag: During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (USD/TRY doubling and tripling) was highly correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, leaving the vulnerable Banking sector at its historical lows.
Structural Break & Equity Hedge (2021–2026): In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite a massive six-fold increase in USD/TRY (from 100 to over 600), BIST indices broke historical patterns and served as a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns.
Industrial Resilience vs. Banking Volatility: Across all analyzed cycles, the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) consistently offered the most stable hedge against currency inflation, while the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) remained the most volatile but staged a dramatic valuation recovery in USD terms post-2021.

title: Data
text:
The Code:
TICKERS <- c(“TRY=X”, “XU100.IS”, “XBANK.IS”, “XUSIN.IS”)
Fetching data using quantmod
prices_lst <- list()
for (t in TICKERS) {
getSymbols(t, from = START_DATE, to = END_DATE, auto.assign = FALSE)
# Extraction of Adjusted Close prices…
}

API Stability: We replaced the TCMB EVDS API with quantmod (Yahoo Finance). EVDS frequently suffers from SSL/TLS handshake failures and connection timeouts on Windows.



Consistency: Fetching all assets (including the USD/TRY exchange rate TRY=X) from a single global source ensures identical timestamps and database formats.
10-Year Horizon: A longer timeframe captures multiple macroeconomic cycles and structural shifts. Yahoo Finance automatically back-adjusts BIST prices for the 2020 denomination (two-zero drop) reform, preserving historical continuity.

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text: This study aims to investigate the correlation between USD/TRY exchange rate movements and the performance of various sectoral indices in Turkey. We are specifically analyzing whether currency volatility serves as a significant driver for returns in the BIST 100 (XU100.IS), Banking (XBANK.IS), and Industrial (XUSIN.IS) sectors.

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text:
Data Set
This study is built upon a comprehensive financial dataset spanning a 20-year period from January 2006 to May 2026. The primary variables analyzed include:

USD/TRY Exchange Rate: The core indicator used to measure currency volatility and macroeconomic stability.
BIST 100 Index (XU100.IS): The main benchmark for the overall performance of the Turkish equity market.
Sectoral Indices: BIST Banking (XBANK.IS) and BIST Industrials (XUSIN.IS) were selected to evaluate varying sectoral sensitivities to currency shocks.


Source & Selection: While the team initially attempted to utilize the Central Bank (TCMB) EVDS system, we pivoted to Yahoo Finance due to the technical complexities of EVDS API integration for a two-decade scope.
Data Preprocessing: Raw time series were cleaned through inner joins to ensure aligned trading days, filtered for missing values (NAs), and transformed into daily log returns for robust statistical analysis.

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text:
Key Takeaways

1. Long-Term Comparative Scope
This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and Borsa Istanbul (BIST) indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to identify structural shifts in market behavior.


2. The 2011–2020 Depreciation Cycle
During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (as shown by the USD/TRY parity doubling and tripling) was generally correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, with the Banking sector consistently being the most vulnerable to exchange rate shocks.


3. Structural Divergence (2021–2026)
In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite the USD/TRY parity increasing six-fold, BIST indices provided a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns for the first time in a decade.


4. Sectoral Sensitivity and Resilience
While the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) typically offered more stability across all cycles, the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) displayed extreme volatility but showed a surprising recovery in USD terms during the 2021–2026 period.


5. Investment Implications
The analysis concludes that while exchange rate spikes historically triggered market sell-offs, the current economic regime has transformed the Turkish equity market into a primary vehicle for capital preservation against extreme currency volatility.

title: Data
text:
The Code:
TICKERS <- c(“TRY=X”, “XU100.IS”, “XBANK.IS”, “XUSIN.IS”)
Fetching data using quantmod
prices_lst <- list()
for (t in TICKERS) {
getSymbols(t, from = START_DATE, to = END_DATE, auto.assign = FALSE)
# Extraction of Adjusted Close prices…
}

API Stability: We replaced the TCMB EVDS API with quantmod (Yahoo Finance). EVDS frequently suffers from SSL/TLS handshake failures and connection timeouts on Windows.



Consistency: Fetching all assets (including the USD/TRY exchange rate TRY=X) from a single global source ensures identical timestamps and database formats.
10-Year Horizon: A longer timeframe captures multiple macroeconomic cycles and structural shifts. Yahoo Finance automatically back-adjusts BIST prices for the 2020 denomination (two-zero drop) reform, preserving historical continuity.

title: Data
text:
2. Multi-Market Synchronization (The inner_join Strategy)
The Code:
Merging multiple datasets into a single tibble
price_data <- prices_lst %>% reduce(inner_join, by = “Date”)

Calendar Mismatch: Forex (TRY=X) trades 24/5 globally and remains open during Turkish local or religious holidays, while Borsa Istanbul (BIST) strictly closes.



The Solution: We used inner_join to keep only the dates common to all assets. If BIST was closed on a holiday, that day is discarded. This ensures our statistical models compare active trading days only.

title: Data
text:
3. Handling Missing Values (drop_na) & Outlier Philosophy
The Code:
Ensuring a clean, gap-free matrix
price_data <- price_data %>%
drop_na()

No Imputation: In finance, filling missing values using averages (imputation) or carrying prices forward is highly discouraged. It artificially dampens volatility and biases daily returns. Removing incomplete rows with drop_na() is the standard, unbiased approach.



Keeping Outliers: While statistical tests flag extreme returns (shocks) as outliers, we did not delete them. In finance, outliers represent real market crises and circuit-breakers. Removing them would destroy the exact macro-shocks we aim to study.

title: Data
text:
4. Mathematical Transformation: Logarithmic Returns
The Code:
Transforming raw prices to stationary log returns
return_data <- price_data %>% mutate(across(starts_with(“Raw_”), ~ log(.x / lag(.x)), .names = “Ret_{.col}”)) %>% rename_with(~ gsub(“Ret_Raw_”, “Ret_”, .x)) %>% drop_na()

Stationarity: Raw asset prices have random walks and trends, violating the core assumptions of regression and correlation. Logarithmic returns (\(R_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})\)) stabilize mean and variance, making the time series stationary.

Time Additivity: Unlike simple percentage returns, the sum of daily log returns over a period exactly equals the total log return of that entire period.
Symmetry: Log returns are symmetric. A \(10\%\) gain followed by a \(10\%\) loss returns the asset to its exact starting value, preventing statistical skewness.

title: Data
text:
5. Multi-Scale Normalization (Cumulative Base-100 Returns)
The Code:
pd_norm <- pd %>% mutate(across(-Date, ~ (.x / first(.x)) * 100))

Scale Incompatibility: You cannot visually compare USD/TRY (ranging between 1.5 to 32+ TL) with the BIST 100 index (ranging in thousands of points) on the same Y-axis.
The Solution: We normalized all assets to a Base-100 index starting on the first day of each period. This scales all asset growth relative to a common starting line (\(Price_{t_0} = 100\)), enabling a fair, percentage-based visual comparison.

title: Data
text:
6. Currency Denomination (USD-Denominated BIST)

The Code:
BIST100_USD = XU100.IS / TRY=X

Eliminating the Inflation Illusion: In high-inflation economies, stock indices in local currency (TRY) almost always trend upward. However, these “rallies” are often nominal illusions driven by currency depreciation.
The Solution: Dividing BIST prices by the USD/TRY rate reveals the USD-denominated (real) value of Turkish equities. This shows whether the market actually grew in global purchasing power or simply inflated.
